coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots/Ruthenium(III) nanocomposites for FRET fluorescence detection and removal of mercury (II) via assembling into nanofibers
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Jian
Zhang, Xin
Zhou, Wanxin
Yao, Renyi
Zhang, Xiyang
Jing, Su
coredata.dc:description: The determination and removal of mercury(II) (Hg2+) are essential for human health and environmental ecosystems. Herein, an ingenious carbon dots (CDs)-based Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) system (N, S-CDs/Ru) was fabricated employing CDs and Ru3+ units as energy-transfer doner/acceptor pairs for visual detection and efficient removal of Hg2+. The treatment of Hg2+ induced a remarkable linear enhancement of the ratiometric fluorescence (F613 nm/F478 nm) with a detection limit (LOD) of 95 nM, along with continuous fluorescence color variations from blue to red. Given that the fluorescence color recognition and processing realized the real-time and rapid quantitation of Hg2+ by paper-based smartphone sensing platform. The mechanistic study revealed that the N/S/O-rich surface of the system enabled the Hg2+-triggered self-assembly from dots to nanofibers, combing with the active FRET process. Also, the efficient removal of Hg2+ with a removal efficiency of ∼98 % and an adsorption capacity of ∼372 mg/g was obtained. Furthermore, it was found that N, S-CDs/Ru loaded commercialized SiO2 or SBA-15 could facilitate the removal of Hg2+ with a removal efficiency over 99 % and an adsorption capacity up to ∼562 mg/g. This study provides a potential strategy for environmental monitoring and remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
FRET ratiometric fluorescence
Hg2+ removal
Self-assembly

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Experimental
Preparation of the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites
Modification of commercialized SiO2 and SBA-15 nanoparticles with N, S-CDs/Ru nanoparticles
Selective detection of Hg2+ with N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites
Determination of Hg2+ in actual samples
Preparation of paper-based smartphone sensing platform with N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites
Determination of Hg2+with paper-based smartphone sensing platform
Removal of Hg2+ in water
Results and discussion
Characterization of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites
Optimization for ratiometric fluorescent detection and removal of Hg2+
Ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detection of Hg2+ based on N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites
The ratiometric fluorescent detection and adsorption mechanism of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites toward Hg2+
Selectivity and anti-interference of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites toward Hg2+
Detection of Hg2+ in actual samples
The performance of paper-based smartphone sensing platform for Hg2+
Removal of Hg2+ in wastewater
Conclusion
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgments
Supplementary data
References

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and size distribution image of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites; (B) SEM image of N, S-CDs/Ru nanofibers after the addition of Hg2+.

(A) XPS spectrum of survey scan; (B)The high-resolution scan of C 1s (C) N 1s (D) O 1s and (E) S 2p of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites; (F) The high-resolution scan of N 1s (G) O 1s (H) S 2p and (I) Hg 4f of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites after the addition of Hg2+, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs/Ru in B–R buffer (pH = 7.0) at different amounts of Hg2+(0–20 μM). (B) The histogram of the normalized fluorescence intensity of F613 nm and F478 nm at varying amounts of Hg2+ (The experiments were performed in triplicate). (C) A linear plot of F613 nm/F478 nm versus Hg2+ concentration ranged from 0 to 12 μM. (D) Fluorescence visualization picture of N, S-CDs/Ru in buffer at different concentrations of Hg2+.
SEM-EDS maps of N, O, S, Hg and Ru elements in the N, S-CDs/Ru + Hg2+ fibers.

(A) FTIR spectra of N, S-CDs/Ru before and after reaction with Hg2+. (B) Spectral overlap between absorption of N, S-CDs/Ru + Hg2+ and emission of the N, S-CDs/Ru. (C) Schematic illustration of the ratiometric fluorescent detection and adsorption for Hg2+.

(A) Fluorescence decay and lifetimes of the N, S-CDs/Ru with or without Hg2+. (B) Fluorescence spectra and (C) Fluorescence visualization picture of the N, S-CDs/Ru after adding various metal ions. (D) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F613 nm/F478 nm) of the N, S-CDs/Ru system in the presence of the test ions (0.2 mM) before and after the treatment of Hg2+ (20 μM). (I) the N, S-CDs/Ru+the tested ions; (II) the N, S-CDs/Ru+the tested ions + Hg2+; 0: Hg2+; 2–28: Fe3+, Mn2+, Eu3+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Ag+, Pb2+, Mg2+, K+, Ni3+, Zn2+ and Ca2+.

(A) Photographs of paper-based N, S-CDs/Ru sensor for visual quantitation of Hg2+ under 365 nm UV light. (B) The diagram of relative parameters of optical primary colors was extracted by color scanning APP. (C) The fitting curve of fluorescence chroma values (R/B) analyzed by smartphone color scanning APP.
The photographs of SiO2/N, S-CDs/Ru and SBA-15/N, S-CDs/Ru power before and after removing of Hg2+ under the natural (above) and UV light (below), respectively.
Schematic illustration of the preparation and application of (A) N, S-CDs/Ru, (B) SiO2/N, S-CDs/Ru and (C) SBA-15/N, S-CDs/Ru.
The adsorption and removal performance of three samples at different Hg2+ concentrations in water.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Data are presented as the mean ± SD from the three independent experiments.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots/Ruthenium(III) nanocomposites for FRET fluorescence detection and removal of mercury (II) via assembling into nanofibers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng, 224051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng, 224051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The determination and removal of mercury(II) (Hg2+) are essential for human health and environmental ecosystems. Herein, an ingenious carbon dots (CDs)-based Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) system (N, S-CDs/Ru) was fabricated employing CDs and Ru3+ units as energy-transfer doner/acceptor pairs for visual detection and efficient removal of Hg2+. The treatment of Hg2+ induced a remarkable linear enhancement of the ratiometric fluorescence (F613 nm/F478 nm) with a detection limit (LOD) of 95 nM, along with continuous fluorescence color variations from blue to red. Given that the fluorescence color recognition and processing realized the real-time and rapid quantitation of Hg2+ by paper-based smartphone sensing platform. The mechanistic study revealed that the N/S/O-rich surface of the system enabled the Hg2+-triggered self-assembly from dots to nanofibers, combing with the active FRET process. Also, the efficient removal of Hg2+ with a removal efficiency of ∼98 % and an adsorption capacity of ∼372 mg/g was obtained. Furthermore, it was found that N, S-CDs/Ru loaded commercialized SiO2 or SBA-15 could facilitate the removal of Hg2+ with a removal efficiency over 99 % and an adsorption capacity up to ∼562 mg/g. This study provides a potential strategy for environmental monitoring and remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution has become a global concern due to its serious environmental toxicity and threat to human health [1]. Among them, mercury ion (Hg2+) is one of the most hazardous pollutants widely released from oil refiners, metal smelters, mining activities and combustion [2]. Bioaccumulation of Hg2+ in the body occurs through the skin, respiration, and food chain, leading to severe diseases such as muscle coordination loss, motion disorders, etc. [3]. Given its wide distribution, high toxicity and bioaccumulation, Hg2+ has been listed as a toxic priority pollutant by the U. S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [4]. Hence, a straightforward approach to be developed for facile, highly sensitive and selective determination, and efficient removal of Hg2+ is urgently required [5].

Ratiometric fluorescence probe takes the ratio of dual-emission intensities as an intrinsic self-calibration for signal correction, insusceptible to the probe concentration, the environmental factors and the instrumental efficiency [6,7]. It is thus considered to be an ideal alternative for highly sensitive and reliable detection [8–11]. FRET serves as a powerful mechanism to construct ratiometric fluorescence probes. By which the analyte shortens the distance between energy donor/acceptor pairs within 10 nm and triggers the non-radiative energy transferring from donor to acceptor with the quenching of donor's fluorescence intensity and an enhancement of receptor intensity. It is liable to take the variation of different fluorescence colors, which is extremely desirable to visual analysis [12]. CDs as newly emerging carbon-based nanomaterials with fascinating merits of high photostability, easy synthesis/surface functionalization and good biocompatibility have attracted many efforts to build.

CDs-based FRET systems as environmental probes. Careful consideration of the extinction coefficients, absorption spectra and emission of CDs, Liu and Hamd-Ghadareh groups adopted the rhodamine derivative/graphene quantum dots or DNA-functionalized carbon dots/AuNPs strategy to construct FRET probe, respectively, where the introduction of Hg2+ caused opposite variations with quenched energy acceptor emission and activated donner emission [13,14]. Wang et al. constructed an FRET probe based on CdSe QDs/g-C3N4 nanocomposites, which turned off the fluorescence emission from the energy doner of CdSe QDs in the presence of Hg2+ [15]. However, since it lacks effective energy donor/acceptor pairs, CDs used to design such probes are still relatively few [16].

The removal of Hg2+ from polluted water is an urgent approach to environmental sustainability [17]. The current research focuses on the adsorption strategy with a series of porous materials such as porous silicas [18], zeolites [19], porous organic polymers [20], and organic-metal frameworks [21]. However, some deficiencies still limit their applications, such as lack of visualization and selectivity, time-consuming adsorption equilibrium, ineffective adsorption capacities, insufficient solid-liquid separation, as well as nonnegligible residuals [22]. To overcome these limitations, few attempts have been made to achieve rapid and efficient synchronously target Hg2+ sensing and removal by introducing the fluorescence signal transduction moiety and highly dense Hg2+ binding sites on the skeleton [23–26].

Herein, an ingenious platform integrated with synchronous visual sensing and efficient removal toward Hg2+ was developed by employing melamine and l-cystine as the N/S sources and high dense Hg2+ binding sites. Under the trigger of Hg2+, the strong Hg2+ complexing affinity of the triazine ring, thiol and carbonyl groups forced the blue fluorescence emission (λem = 478 nm) of CDs to rapidly self-assemble to produce hybrid nanofiber architecture with a red fluorescence emission variation (λem = 613 nm) via FRET, exhibiting a typical ratiometric fluorescence response. Such unique properties endowed it as a paper-based sensing platform for feasible and rapid quantitative analysis of Hg2+ by integrating a smartphone with a color detector APP [27–29]. Moreover, to further improve the Hg2+ removal capacity, N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites were grafted into commercialized SiO2 and SBA-15. As a result, a prominent increase in their removal efficiency and adsorption capacity of Hg2+ was successfully realized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
ce:section.ce:para: Materials and instruments were listed in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The dual emissive N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites were fabricated by a traditional hydrothermal approach with the melamine and l-cystine as the nitrogen and sulfur sources. Specifically, the mixture containing 0.2522 g (2.0 mmol) melamine and 0.2403 g (1.0 mmol) l-cystine was dissolved in 28 mL deionized water. Under stirring, 0.0420 g (0.20 mmol) 1,10-phenanthroline-5,6-dione dissolved in 2 mL ethanol solution and 0.0523 g (0.20 mmol) RuCl3·3H2O power were separately added into the above-mixed solution. A 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave was used to carbonize the mixed solution at 180 °C for 4h after mixing it for 10 min. When it was cooled to room temperature naturally, the pure N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites power was obtained by procedures following centrifugation at 10000 r/min for 20 min, dialysis with DI water for 48 h, and freeze-drying at −30 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of commercialized SiO2 and SBA-15 nanoparticles with N, S-CDs/Ru nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: 0.05 g commercialized SiO2 was added to 10 mL DI water, and then the solution was sonicated for 30 min. After dispersion, 10 mL of dispersion solution containing with 0.05 g N, S-CDs/Ru was slowly dripped into the above mixture under a shaker stirring and kept stirring for another 8 h. The final SiO2/N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites power was obtained by procedures following centrifugation at 10000 r/min for 20 min, washing with DI water and drying in the air (Scheme 1 ).
SBA-15/N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites were prepared in a manner similar to SiO2/N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites, using a dispersion solution of SBA-15 instead of SiO2 (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of Hg2+ with N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: For the selectivity experiment, 3 mL of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites (2 mg/mL) Britton Robinson buffer (B–R buffer, 10 mM, pH 7.0) was added to a series of 5 mL centrifugal tubes, followed by adding different kinds of metal ions respectively, including Fe3+, Mn2+, Eu3+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Ag+, Pb2+, Mg2+, K+, Ni3+, Cu2+, Ca2+, and Hg2+. The final concentration of all ions was excess to 20 μM. After reacting for 1 min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solutions were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 400 nm. For the quantitative analysis of Hg2+, the fluorescence emission intensity ratio (F613 nm/F478 nm) was constructed as a standard curve with different levels of Hg2+(ranging from 0 to 20 μM). Based on this linear curve, the content of detected Hg2+ could be evaluated. All color changes were captured under a 365 nm UV lamp. For the colorimetric detection of Hg2+, the analytes containing each metal ion were prepared in a similar method as described above.
Their resulting absorbance spectra were then measured using UV–vis spectrophotometry. All measurements were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the practicality of N, S-CDs/Ru ratiometric fluorescent probe for Hg2+ detection, standard addition methods were carried out to measure the concentration of Hg2+ in tap water and wastewater environments, respectively. The tap water and wastewater samples were collected from our lab building (Yancheng, China) and Chemical Enterprise (Yancheng, China), respectively. The prepared tap water samples were used for subsequent analysis without any pretreatment. For wastewater samples assay, they were filtered with 0.22 μm membrane and centrifuged for 20 min with 10000 r/min to remove large particles. Then a 10-fold dilution of the supernatants was performed with 10 mM buffer. The N, S-CDs/Ru power was dispersed in 3 mL above samples (2 mg/mL, tap water or wastewater samples), and then the standard solutions with various concentrations of Hg2+ were added. Afterwards, the fluorescence spectra measurement was conducted after incubation for 1 min of the resulting solutions. The detailed test conditions correspond to the above description.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of paper-based smartphone sensing platform with N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The common fiber filter paper was selected to fabricate paper-based containers with a CAD program to design the device model and the thermal wax printing technology to print the device. Then it was immersed in the solution of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites (∼2 mg/mL). After 10 min, the paper platform was taken out and dried at 50 °C under vacuum for 30 min. The dried paper-based platform loaded with N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites uniformly was obtained for further Hg2+ determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+with paper-based smartphone sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: To quantitatively determine the concentration of Hg2+, 10 μL of Hg2+ solution was separately dropped to the as-prepared papers with different concentrations. After 1 min, the fluorescence photographs were taken by a smartphone under a 365 nm UV lamp. Their color parameters, including red (R) and blue (B) were directly obtained by a color detector APP (Color Picker) equipped in the smartphone, and their linear curve between the ratio values of R/B and Hg2+ concentration was constructed. Based on this linear equation, upon capturing the R and B parameters of samples, the concentration of detected Hg2+ could be quickly calculated by a CalcuCrvFit APP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of Hg2+ in water
ce:section.ce:para:
The removal performances of N, S-CDs/Ru, SiO2/N, S-CDs/Ru and SBA-15/N, S-CDs/Ru composites for Hg2+ were determined by ICP-MS. Typically, under stirring, an aliquot of N, S-CDs/Ru, SiO2/N, S-CDs/Ru and SBA-15/N, S-CDs/Ru composites (20 mg) were separately added into 10 mL of each of different concentrations (2.49, 12.51, 312.48, 1249.91, 3749.74, 5626.10 and 6188.69 μM) for 5 min at room temperature. After the filtration through filtration apparatus (pore size 0.22 μm), the concentrations of the residual Hg2+ in the solutions were determined accordingly. All experiments were performed in triplicate. Afterwards, their precipitations were dried in an oven at 50 °C and were taken photographs by a smartphone under natural and UV light, respectively. The adsorption capacity (Q
e , mg/g) and removal efficiency (E, %) of Hg2+ were further calculated by the equations listed below, where V, m, C0 and Ce represent the volume of the solution, the quality of adsorbent, the initial and final concentrations of Hg2+, respectively:
Adsorption
capacity
Q e
=
(
C 0
‐ Ce )
· V / m
Removal
efficiency
E
( % )
=
(
(
C 0
‐ Ce )
/
C 0
)
× 100
%

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites were explored using TEM images. Fig. 1 A clearly revealed that those nanoparticles had a well-homogeneous dispersed spherical morphology with a distribution range from 33 to 48 nm, fitting with normal distribution. With an average size of 40 nm, the diameter of these N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites was approximately sixteen times larger than that of our previously reported N, S-co-doped CDs (∼2.5 nm) obtained from melamine and l-cystine [30]. It might be close to the aggregation effect from Ru3+coordinating with surface groups on CDs and the auxiliary ligand of 1,10-phenanthroline-5,6-dione.

The surface groups and chemical composition of the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites were investigated by FTIR and XPS technology, respectively. The detailed discussion was provided in the Supporting Information. All results of FT-IR and XPS spectra clearly demonstrated that N and S elements have been perfectly doped in the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites, and Ru have been successfully coordinated with Ru–O and Ru–N bonds. Furthermore, N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites were composed of sp2 carbon core and equipped with abundant functional groups on their surfaces, such as C=N, C–N, C=O, COOH, OH, NH2, C–S, triazine ring, Ru3+ unit and so on. As a result, these nanomaterials were endowed with a considerable amount of luminescence centers and adequate binding sites for self-assembly with Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for ratiometric fluorescent detection and removal of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the best performance of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites for the sensing and removal of Hg2+, several important parameters such as excitation wavelength, pH and reaction time were optimized. The choice of excitation wavelength plays a vital role, since the excited energy must simultaneously reach the excited state of CDs and Ru3+ emitters on the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites, and stimulate sensitive fluorescence color variations with the addition of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. S1A, the emission from N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites exhibited a distinct excitation-dependent emission property between blue and red emission regions when the exciting wavelength was changed from 340

to 500 nm, consistent with two primary luminescent components, excitation-dependent CDs and excitation-independent Ru3+ unit. In this process, the fluorescence intensity at 478 nm decreased and at 613 nm increased gradually, accompanied by fluorescence color changes from blue to red (Fig. S1B). When the excited wavelength was located at 400 nm, the fluorescence intensity and the ratio value of F613 nm/F478 nm were more favorable for sensitive fluorescence color changes from blue to red for colorimetric detection. Then, the pH of the solution and reaction time were optimized. As shown in Fig. S2, the system displayed the best performance for Hg2+ in pH = 7.0 B–R buffer and the entire consumed time within 1 min. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites in pH = 7.0 B–R buffer within 30 days could still be maintained at approximately 93 %, suggesting a suitable fluorescence stability of the probe (Fig. S3). Hence, 400 nm of excited wavelength, pH = 7.0 of B–R buffer and 1min of reaction time were adopted as the optimized experimental parameters for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detection of Hg2+ based on N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Under the aforementioned optimal parameters, the ratiometric fluorescent response of N, S-CDs/Ru platform towards Hg2+ was comparably explored. The standard solution of Hg2+ with different levels was added in the N, S-CDs/Ru dispersion solution, and the relevant fluorescence spectra were determined after 1 min. The fluorescence spectra showed two distinct emission peaks at 478 nm (strong) and 613 nm (weak) when excited at 400 nm (the PLQY at 613 nm was 8.14 %), and the predominant blue fluorescence was observed. As shown in Fig. 3A, with increasing Hg2+concentration (0–20 μM), the fluorescence intensity at 613 nm increased while those at 478 nm decreased constantly, and reached saturation at about 16 μM of Hg2+. Notably, during this course, the proportion of red fluorescence emission continued to rise and the highest triple increasement of the ratio value F613 nm/F478 nm was achieved (the PLQY at 613 nm was 20.38 %). It was companied by the visualized fluorescence color gradually varied from blue to red (Fig. 3B and D). Furthermore, a satisfactory linear relationship between the Hg2+ concentration (0–12 μM) and ratio value of F613 nm/F478 nm could achieve (Fig. 3C). The calibration equation was y = 0.0668C(Hg 2+ , μM) + 0.5620 (R2 = 0.9921). The y represented the value of the F613 nm/F478 nm. Based on the rule 3σ/k, the LOD was calculated to be 95 nM. Although the LOD was comparable to previously reported Hg2+ ratio fluorescence sensors, the N, S-CDs/Ru sensor avoided a complex synthesis process and a time-consuming reaction with Hg2+(Table S1). More importantly, in comparison with the single fluorescence response of brightness variation, this

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescent detection and adsorption mechanism of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites toward Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the adsorption and detection process, the possible mechanism was discussed. As described in Fig. 1B, the introduction of Hg2+ rapidly drove the self-assembly with functional groups on the N, S-CDs/Ru surface to form uniformly nanofibers with an average diameter of ∼0.47 μm and average length of ∼20.64 μm. The EDS mapping images could further verify the outstanding retention of Hg2+ by the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites (Fig. 4). To further identify the possible adsorption sites of Hg2+ bonded to the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites, XPS and FT-IR spectra were analyzed before and after the Hg2+ treatment. Strong Hg signals detected from the XPS survey spectrum of N, S-CDs/Ru + Hg2+ clearly confirm the adsorption of Hg, agreeing well with the EDS results. That high-resolution XPS spectra of Hg 4f obviously exhibited doublet-peaks corresponding to Hg–O and Hg–S bonds at 100.8(Hg–O, Hg 4f7/2), 105.1(Hg–O, Hg 4f5/2),100.2(Hg–S, Hg 4f7/2) and 104.4 eV(Hg–S, Hg 4f5/2)(Fig. 2 I ) [31]. O 1s and S 2p XPS spectra appeared one new peak at 532.7 and 162.1 eV in agreement with the O–Hg and S–Hg bonds, respectively (Fig. 2G and H) [32]. Also, the binding energy of pyridinic N (398.4 eV), C=O (532.1 eV) and C–S (164.3 eV) reduced to 398.0, 531.8 and 164.0 eV, respectively, indicating that the N (pyridinic N), O (C=O) and S (C–S) atoms had direct interaction with Hg2+ in the adsorption process(Fig. 2F-H) [33]. In terms of FT-IR spectra of N, S-CDs/Ru + Hg2+, the bands ascribed to C=O (1683 cm−1) and C=N (1648 cm−1) shifted to 1657 and 1628 cm−1, transferring lone pair electrons of O and N to the empty orbits of Hg2+ [34]. Meanwhile, the characteristic bands assigned to the triazine ring (1543, 1459 cm−1) and C–S (1000 cm−1) got stronger and red-shifted to 1560, 1468 and 1026 cm−1 accordingly (Fig. 5 A). All these results further confirmed that the formation of stable substances by complexing between N/S/O and Hg2+ after the addition of Hg2+.

Subsequently, in terms of the energy transfer process, the absorption bands at 450 nm enhanced with increased amounts of Hg2+, inducing the effect overlap profile between Ru3+ unit absorption and CDs unit emission spectra (Fig. 5B). Thereby, the effect FRET process from CDs unit to Ru3+ unit was established. Considering that an remarkable FRET occurrence generally relies on a typical range of roughly 1–10 nm and the resonance effect between the FRET donor/acceptor pairs [35], in the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites architecture, we speculated that the coordination of Hg2+ with functional groups on the surface narrowed the distance between CDs and Ru3+ unit during the self-assembling process (Fig. 5C). Such shortened distance and spectral transition gave rise to the active FRET process, where the CDs served as the FRET donor, Ru3+ unit was the FRET acceptor. Moreover, the proposed mechanism was well supported by fluorescence lifetime decay. The addition of Hg2+ increased the average lifetime of the acceptor (λem = 613 nm) from 3.45 to 3.91 ns, attributed to Hg2+-induced FRET process (Fig. 6 A) [36]. As a result, the blue emission profile of CDs was gradually quenched, whereas the red emission profile of Ru3+ units was enhanced with the addition of the levels of Hg2+. Such dramatic signal response by oppositely turned dual fluorescence, showing the continuous color changes from blue to red, favorable for visualized detection of Hg2+. Moreover, apart from the FRET process, the electrons of excited-state CDs transfer to the empty orbital of Hg2+ may simultaneously be occurred in the quenching event, leading to intensifying a blue fluorescence profile decrease.

Therefore, the splendid selectivity, adsorption and sensitivity of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites were attributed to the stronger affinity of the –COOH, C=N, C–S, C=O and triazine ring-riched on the surface of nanocomposites toward Hg2+ and the unique FRET process caused by Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites toward Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the selectivity of the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites towards Hg2+, some potential interfering substances of metal ions were respectively added in the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites dispersion solution. As depicted in Fig. 6B and C, except that the Ag+ could simultaneously quench the fluorescence signals at 613 nm and 478 nm, because of the electron transfer between the formed silver-thiotriazinone complexes and N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites, there was a negligible fluorescence resultant values or color changes after treatment of other potential interfering ions, even in larger quantities and over longer reaction time. Only Hg2+ could trigger a significant increment of ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric responses. Additionally, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the N, S- CDs/Ru system also changed obviously after adding different concentrations of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. S6A, the absorbance peaks at 272 nm and 453 nm enhanced gradually with increasing Hg2+ concentrations. There were no noticeable absorbance changes in other metal ions (Fig. S6B). These fluorescence and UV–vis results demonstrated the great selectivity of the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites toward Hg2+ in contrast to other potential interfering species.

Besides the sensitivity and the selectivity, the anti-interference is also a critical parameter for evaluating the sensor performance. As shown in Fig. 6D, the evident ratiometric fluorescence still could be observed in the co-existing environment of Hg2+ and other metal ions, owing to the stronger affinity of Hg2+ to N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites than others. These results meant that N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites had a high degree of specificity for Hg2+, and showed great potentials to be applied in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the practical application of our developed ratiometric fluorescent sensor in actual samples, Hg2+ was detected in tap water and wastewater samples. Both the results of tap water and wastewater samples were listed in Table S2. The Hg2+ concentration in the above samples was not

detectable by the proposed sensor, for the concentration of Hg2+ in these samples might be less than the LOD of this approach. To further evaluate the accuracy of the sensor, we calculated the recovery of samples by the standard addition method. After spiking different concentrations of Hg2+ (0.01, 0.10, 1.00, 10.00 μM) into the tap water and wastewater samples, the recovery results ranged from 93.00 to 107.70 %, and the RSD values for three measurements were less than 5.00 %, manifesting the high reliability and feasibility of the proposed method. These results indicated their potential applications in ratiometric fluorescence detection in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of paper-based smartphone sensing platform for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Portable and low-cost test strips were constructed for instant on-site visual detection of Hg2+ by immobilizing N, S-CDs/Ru on filter paper. With different concentrations of Hg2+ added and reacted for 1 min, an obvious fluorescence color transition from blue to red of the test paper could be observed with the naked eye under a 365 nm UV light (Fig. 7 A). Considering that each concentration of Hg2+ corresponded to each fluorescence colors, it was feasible to rapid the semi-quantitative and qualitative determination of Hg2+.

To further made full use of a paper-based platform and realize the rapid and accurate qualitative detection of Hg2+ on site, the fluorescence photometric analysis was approached with a color scanning APP, F Color Picker, on the smartphone. When a certain amount of Hg2+ was added to the paper-based platform, their relative parameters of optical primary colors (RGB, R for red, G for Green, B for blue) were extracted by color scanning APP (Fig. 7B). The linear analysis of the R/B values and the corresponding Hg2+ concentrations was carried out using a CalcuCrvFit APP. The linear equation was listed as follows: R/G = 0.0556C(Hg 2+ , μM) + 0.4490, R2 = 0.9950 (0–16 μM) (Fig. 7C and S6). Upon a test Hg2+ solution (0–16 μM) was dropped into the paper-based platform and reacted with 1 min, the accurate concentration was quickly exported after operating color scanning APP and CalcuCrvFit APP (Fig. S6). This paper-based smartphone sensing platform based on N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites afforded an accurate and intelligent method for the determination of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of Hg2+ in wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: Given a large amount of fluffy precipitation in N, S-CDs/Ru dispersion solution (2.0 mg/L) produced upon the addition of Hg2+(≥16 μM) (Fig. S4), the Hg2+ removal capacity of the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites in the water was explored. The removal performances of the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites were investigated with different concentrations of Hg2+ and their corresponding adsorption ability and removal efficiency were shown in Table 1 . The results indicated that the Hg2+ removal efficiency of N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites was increased with the increment of Hg2+ concentrations (2.49–3749.74 μM), and could achieve up to 98.91 % for the high Hg2+ adsorption capacity (371.99 mg/g), comparable to those reported Hg2+-harvest materials (Table S3). The SEM-EDS mapping images could further verify the retention of Hg2+ with a large number by the N, S- CDs/Ru nanocomposites. On the samples after being treated with Hg2+, wide distribution of Hg signals was clearly observed, which accounts for 3.95 % of total elemental content (Fig. S5), much larger than that of reported cellulose nanocrystal-carbon dots (1.9 %) [25]. These great Hg2+-removal efficiency and capacity should be attributed to their powerful Hg2+-chelating ability of C=N, C–S and C=O-rich structures in nanocomposites, leading to the formation of the extremely stable coordination compounds and self-assembling into the tricky nanofibers.

To further improve the Hg2+-removal performance, the compounds denoted as SiO2/N, S-CDs/Ru and SBA-15/N, S-CDs/Ru were prepared with the commercial silica gels and SBA-15 serving as substrates to load with the N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites on their surfaces. Their actual competence of the removal capabilities and efficiencies was evaluated, listed in Table 1. It was found that this instrument has more prominent Hg2+-trapping capabilities in comparison with that of free N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites, owing to their favorable adsorption capacity of substrates, contact area and solid-liquid separation. The highest removal efficiencies of SiO2/N, S-CDs/Ru and SBA-15/N, S-CDs/Ru reached up to 99.60 % and 99.66 %, corresponding adsorption capacities of 562.00 and 562.35 mg/g, respectively, much higher than that of their substrates alone (Table S4). There was almost no Hg2+ leaching from the filtration apparatus. Their colors of filter residues turned from pale yellow to orange and from blue to red under visible and ultraviolet light, respectively, caused by the abundant chelate of Hg2+ (Fig. 8 ). Similar to that of the free N, S-CDs/Ru, the rapid self-assembly of Hg2+ with functional groups on these nanocomposites loaded substrates were also occurred during encountering with Hg2+, forming aggregated SiO2/N, S-CDs/Ru nanospheres and SBA-15/N, S-CDs/Ru nanorods with increased diameter, respectively (Fig. S8). Furthermore, the wide distribution of Hg signals was clearly observed in the SEM-EDS maps of their filter residues, further verifying their outstanding retention of Hg2+ (Figs. S9 and S10). The adsorption/desorption experiments were carried out by regenerating of the N, S-CDs/Ru through treatment with 1 mol L−1 HCl and thiourea solution for 8 h. After five desorption cycles, the Hg2+ adsorption performance of the three adsorbents did not significant changes during the repeated adsorption-desorption cycles (Fig. S11). Therefore, these adsorbents exhibited good recyclability and could be extensively applied for real applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, N, S-CDs/Ru nanocomposites were constructed by a traditional hydrothermal approach with the Ru3+ unit as the energy accepter/skeletal connector and phenanthroline-5,6-dione as an auxiliary ligand. The presence of Hg2+ rapidly drove the self-assembly with abundant N/S/O functional groups on the N, S-CDs/Ru surface to form uniform nanofibers and induced energy transfer from CDs to Ru3+ unit, giving rise to active FRET process. It thus showed vivid fluorescence color changes from blue to red as the increase of Hg2+ concentration with the rise of F613nm/F478nm. In comparison with other metal ions, only Hg2+ could trigger distinct fluorescence color variations with the LOD as 95 nM. Given that, through the integration of RGB value analysis APP on smart phone and N, S-CDs/Ru immobilized paper-based sensor, Hg2+ could be intelligently qualitatively identified. Moreover, such abundant binding sites and contact areas enabled N, S-CDs/Ru efficient adsorption of Hg2+ with the removal efficiency of 98.91 % with an adsorption capacity of 371.99 mg/g in a short time. Furthermore, N, S-CDs/Ru loaded SiO2 and SBA-15 could further improve efficiency for removal of Hg2+ with an adsorption capacity of 99.60 % and 99.66 % and an adsorption capacity of 562.00 and 562.35 mg/g, respectively. It promised vast potential applications in intelligent environmental monitoring and efficient wastewater treatment fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Jian Qu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xin Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Software. Wanxin Zhou: Investigation, Formal analysis. Renyi Yao: Visualization, Investigation. Xiyang Zhang: Validation, Data curation. Su Jing: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by funding for Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20191041), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22175090) and the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2021712).
